data.title: Trump's new battleship project risks national security
data.description:
President Trump says modern U.S. ships are “ugly” — not ugly in the damage they can do to the enemy, but aesthetically ugly to what he believes is an expert eye. Hence, the commander-in-chief has directed design and construction of a whole new fleet battleships named — you guessed it — the “Trump Class.”

This idea would be laughable if it weren’t so costly and dangerous.

In announcing his huge battleship initiative, surrounded by the gold trappings of Mar-a-Lago, Trump said he would help design the ships himself because he is a “very aesthetic person.”

This emphasis on aesthetics over stealth, survivability and strength in new ship construction surely made eyes roll at the Pentagon. But a more fundamental and important question is whether battleships make sense any longer as a national defense investment. And make no mistake, this investment would cost American taxpayers hundreds of billions, siphon resources away from the Navy’s most urgent needs, and preoccupy America’s shipbuilding capacity.

Aesthetics are not a military strategy when deterrence, national security and sailors’ lives are at stake.

Battleships became obsolete decades ago. They are expensive, manpower-intensive, and a highly vulnerable target in an era defined by precision missiles, submarines, drones, and hypersonic weapons. They are a throwback to pre-World War II days, when gunboat imperialism was great power norm. Ironically, no new battleship was necessary for Trump’s recent gunboat initiative in the Caribbean.

Ukraine’s 2022 sinking of Russia’s cruiser Moskva should have erased any lingering illusion or nostalgia for massive surface combatants. Bigger ships aren’t always stronger ships — they are often just easier targets.

As for industrial capacity, reviving battleship construction would impose additional huge burdens on shipyards already stretched to the breaking point. Our industrial base is struggling to produce enough munitions, rehabilitate aging boats, and build new destroyers and submarines required to deter China and Russia. Our magazines are dangerously thin.

Indeed, General Dynamics and Huntington Ingalls Industries — likely lead contractors for a new battleship fleet — are under immense pressure to deliver both Virginia class and new Columbia class submarines, presently behind schedule. For assuring sufficient undersea attack and nuclear deterrent, these submarines are absolutely vital. And the two companies are also producing the Navy’s next generation frigates. Adding battleships to this workload doesn’t expand capacity, it cannibalizes it.

Today’s Navy is doing the most challenging, significant work ever — projecting power against rivals like China and Russia, enforcing sanctions, stopping illicit trafficking, deterring nuclear attacks, and much more. A new fleet of battleships costing hundreds of billions and burdening our defense industrial capacity adds no strategic value worth the sacrifice. A battleship duplicates strike capabilities already provided by submarines, destroyers and aircraft, and actually presents serious vulnerabilities and dangers to our sailors and our nation.

We should be serious about deterring or winning the next war, not the ones fought in the last century. To strengthen deterrence and safeguard our national security, decisions must be made to maximize military power and cost effectiveness, not personal aesthetic pleasure or vanity. Gold plating may be fine for interior decoration, but not for the Navy.

Richard Blumenthal is the senior U.S. senator from Connecticut and a member of the Senate Armed Services Committee.

data.title: Trump endorses Duffy son-in-law in Wisconsin race
data.description:
President Trump endorsed Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy’s son-in-law Michael Alfonso in the GOP primary to succeed Rep. Tom Tiffany (R-Wis.) on Tuesday. 

“Michael is the son-in-law of our GREAT Secretary of Transportation, Sean Duffy, and the wonderful Rachel Campos-Duffy, of FoxNews, one of the most popular (and talented!) anchors in all of Television,” Trump wrote on his Truth Social platform. “His family has been fierce advocates for our Movement to, MAKE AMERICA GREAT AGAIN!” 

Trump lauded Alfonso, who’s married to Duffy’s daughter Evita, calling him a “MAGA Warrior” and a “young ‘STAR.’” 

Alfonso, whose campaign website says he has a background in the construction and concrete industry before becoming a producer for “The Dan Bongino Show,” said in a statement on X that it was the “greatest honor” to receive the president’s backing. 

“He is truly the greatest president of all time, and I pledge to be a steadfast MAGA warrior for the people of Wisconsin’s 7th District,” Alfonso added. 

Alfonso is among a handful of Republicans vying for Tiffany’s seat in northwest Wisconsin. Tiffany is running for Wisconsin governor this year and received Trump’s endorsement on Tuesday. 

Duffy held the seat prior to Tiffany, later joining Fox Business to co-host “The Bottom Line.” 

Other Republicans running for Tiffany’s seat includes Jessi Ebben, who works for a Wisconsin-based manufacturer, private wealth advisor and former U.S. hostage in Iran Kevin Hermening and businessman and corporate lawyer Paul Wassgren. 

Attorney Ginger Murray, former state Rep. Fred Clark and small businessowner Chris Armstrong are all running on the Democratic side. 

Tiffany’s district went for Trump by more than 22 points in November, according to The Downballot, making whoever wins the GOP primary the heavy favorite to win in November.

data.title: Trump claims without evidence Omar 'probably had herself sprayed'
data.description:
President Trump, without evidence, suggested Rep. Ilhan Omar (D-Minn.) staged an incident during her town hall Tuesday night when a man sprayed her with an unknown substance.

“I don’t think about her. I think she’s a fraud,” Trump told ABC News. “She probably had herself sprayed, knowing her.”

When asked if he had seen video of the incident, the president said, “I haven’t seen it. No, no. I hope I don’t have to bother.”

A livestream of the town hall showed the man standing and yelling at Omar, who was calling for Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem to resign or face impeachment after two fatal shootings involving immigration enforcement officers in Minneapolis. The suspect was tackled and arrested after spraying the lawmaker.

Omar continued with the town hall after the incident, saying, “These f—ing a—holes are not going to get away with it.”

She struck an even more defiant tone in remarks after the event.

“I’ve survived war and I’m definitely going to survive intimidation or whatever these people think they can throw at me, because I’m built that way,” the lawmaker said.

The Minnesota Democrat wrote later Tuesday on social platform X, “I’m ok. I’m a survivor so this small agitator isn’t going to intimidate me from doing my work.”

“I don’t let bullies win,” she added. “Grateful to my incredible constituents who rallied behind me. Minnesota strong.”

Trump has frequently launched xenophobic attacks on Omar, the lone Somali American in Congress. Hours before Tuesday’s incident, he criticized her again during remarks from Iowa.

“She comes from a country that’s a disaster,” the president told a crowd. “It’s considered, I think, the worst [country]. It’s not even a country.”

Minnesota Police confirmed in a statement to The Hill that they observed the man, identified as 55-year-old Anthony Kazmierczak, using a syringe to spray an unknown liquid on Omar.

“The man was immediately arrested by MPD officers and booked into Hennepin County Jail for 3rd degree assault. Representative Omar was uninjured and resumed speaking at the event. Minneapolis Forensic Scientists responded to process the scene,” the spokesperson said.

U.S. Capitol Police (USCP) called the incident “an unacceptable decision that will be met with swift justice” in a statement. 

“We are grateful for the rapid response of onsite security and our local law enforcement partners,” USCP continued. “We are now working with our federal partners to see this man faces the most serious charges possible to deter this kind of violence in our society.” 

Multiple GOP members of Congress, including Reps. Mark Alford (Mo.), Mike Lawler (N.Y.) and Nancy Mace (S.C.), denounced the incident.

“What happened at Rep. Ilhan Omar’s town hall — where she was sprayed with a substance during the event — is completely unacceptable,” Lawler wrote on X. “Regardless of political views, we should all agree this behavior crosses a line and cannot be tolerated.”

USCP also said Tuesday that the department has investigated nearly 15,000 “concerning statements behaviors, and communications” directed against members of Congress, their families, staff and the Capitol. In 2024, USCP investigated nearly 9,500 such cases.

data.title: NYC Mayor Zohran Mamdani 'commends' anti-ICE protesters who took over hotel lobby
data.description:
TRIBECA, Manhattan (WPIX) – New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani commended the protesters who took over the lobby of a Hilton hotel on Tuesday to demonstrate against ICE.

“Mayor Mamdani commends the protestors who exercised their right to protest against ICE today. As he has said, ICE is a rogue agency that has repeatedly carried out cruel, inhumane, and lawless raids and arrests of American citizens,” his spokesperson said. “The Mayor is also pleased with the NYPD’s response to the protest and that today’s demonstration concluded without violence.”

Around 70 people were arrested during the protest, held for almost an hour at a Hilton hotel in Tribeca, according to a local immigration group. It’s believed that ICE agents are being housed at the location.

The NYPD hasn’t confirmed how many people were arrested.

The Department of Homeland Security previously condemned the Hilton franchise after owners of a Minneapolis hotel canceled reservations for ICE agents. Hilton responded, saying the actions were “not reflective of Hilton values of being welcoming to all.”

Charges against the protesters have not been revealed. The NYPD told Nexstar’s WPIX that its officers were working to see if any of the protesters had outstanding warrants before formally filing charges.

The protest comes after two federal immigration agents shot and killed ICU nurse Alex Pretti over the weekend. Pretty is the second person shot and killed by immigration officers in Minneapolis.

data.title: Elie Wiesel's son discusses new doc on his dad, the Ye apology and loving 'The Producers'
data.description:
The son of Elie Wiesel says a new documentary about the Nobel Peace Prize winner comes as some Americans are operating with an “activated ignorance” about what’s going on in their country and around the world.

For years, Elisha Wiesel told ITK in an interview, “countless film studios” had inquired about a potential adaptation of “Night,” the groundbreaking memoir by his father about his experience at the infamous Nazi concentration camp Auschwitz.

“My father had been very specific with us that that was something he never wanted,” Elisha Wiesel, 53, said.

“He never wanted another creative voice to get between him and his reader,” he added.

“Nonetheless, my mom and I felt, with all that inquiry, that it did make sense to have something on the screen, that this was such an important medium, and if we did it about his life story, as opposed to just ‘Night,’ that that would still be in keeping with his wishes.”

The result is the documentary “American Masters — Elie Wiesel: Soul on Fire,” which premiered this week on PBS and is available to stream online on the TV network’s website and app.  

PBS called the Oren Rudavsky-directed project — which was released to coincide with Holocaust Remembrance Day on Tuesday — an “intimate portrait” of Elie Wiesel, narrated in the prolific author’s own voice. The film features interviews, archival and personal family footage, along with hand-painted animations. 

Elie Wiesel died in 2016 at 87.

“We were driven by a desire to, I think, remind those who already loved my father just how interesting and brilliant his life was and also to reach new audiences,” Elisha Wiesel said. 

Elisha Wiesel weighed in on premiering the documentary amid a heated political climate, and at a time when antisemitic incidents in the U.S. reached an all-time high in recent years. More than 10,000 antisemitic incidents occurred in the country during a roughly one-year period from 2023-24, according to the Anti-Defamation League. 

Asked what his father might make of the current moment, Wiesel said, “I’m always very gun shy of trying to say what my father would have said. There are very few privileges to being dead, but one of them is not having words put into your mouth.”

“But I would say, if you’ve read [‘Outliers’ author] Malcolm Gladwell and you know about the 10,000 hours as the mark for mastery, I’ve spent my 10,000 hours with my dad. So I feel confident telling you what I feel, and you can extrapolate from that,” he continued.

“I feel like we’ve gone straight past this concept of indifference. If you look at some of the most tried and true quotes that you’ll see attached to almost anything these days, you’ll see my father’s quote that indifference is the worst evil: ‘The opposite of love is not hate, it’s indifference.’ So everybody needs to have a point of view,” Wiesel said.

“Well, we’ve kind of gone way past I think everybody being indifferent. Our problem today is everybody has a point of view. Everybody’s really chiming in, even if they have absolutely zero information or perspective on what’s going on thousands of miles away,” he added.

“So I feel like we’ve moved past indifference to very activated ignorance. And I feel like that’s really the problem of our era right now, where we are.”

Wiesel also offered his thoughts on Ye, the rapper formerly known as Kanye West, who on Monday issued an apology after years of making inflammatory, antisemitic remarks and embracing the Nazi swastika.

In a letter published in The Wall Street Journal, the performer said he “lost touch with reality” following a brain injury and a bipolar disorder diagnosis, writing, “I am not a Nazi or an antisemite. I love Jewish people.”

“I don’t know Kanye. I think that that’s a very personal issue. I don’t know his medical situation. I don’t know what he’s capable of,” Wiesel said.

“But I think that Judaism does believe in a concept of teshuvah, of repentance, that it is something that one can try to do. But it’s very difficult when you defame an entire group of people to walk that back and undo the damage you’ve done.”

Wiesel recounted a famous Hasidic story in which, after a man spread lies about someone, he went to a rabbi seeking forgiveness and a way to redeem himself.

“The rabbi takes a pillowcase that’s full of feathers, and he rips it, and the feathers fly –— borne by the wind — across the entire landscape. And the rabbi says, ‘Go retrieve all of those feathers,'” Wiesel said. 

“That’s the metaphor for how hard it is to undo the damage when you really savagely attack an entire group of people and spread lies and misinformation about them.”

Wiesel described his father, who was born in Romania and became an American citizen in 1963, as a “very deep patriot” who “really loved this country.” And there was also a playful side to him, too.

“He loved the film ‘The Producers,'” Wiesel said of the 1967 satirical comedy about a Broadway production that lauded Nazi leaders. 

“You would think that to hear the song ‘Springtime for Hitler’ be sung, he might be offended today, like in today’s world where everybody’s so easily offended,” Wiesel said. 

“But my father would laugh out loud, big belly laughs. He loved good humor.”

Just as important as Holocaust education was to his father through his writing, Wiesel said, his legacy is also marked by the “choices that he made as a human being — the fact that he chose to overcome his darkness, and actually have a family and have a child.”

“My father went through one of the darkest experiences that we can contemplate… and that he came out the other end, not with a chip on his shoulder, not with ideas of who he wanted to attack because of that. Instead, he came out with a desire to live and he wanted to share that desire,” Wiesel said.

“And I hope people see that infectious joy that really marked him.”

data.title: Watch live: Rubio testifies before Senate on Trump admin moves in Venezuela
data.description:
Secretary of State Marco Rubio will testify Wednesday morning before the Senate Foreign Relations Committee on the Trump administration’s operations in Venezuela, and the capture and ouster of embattled leader Nicolás Maduro.

Lawmakers on both sides of the aisle have expressed concerns over the administration’s moves in the South American country, including President Trump’s vow to run Venezuela and his plans for the nation’s robust oil reserves.

Senators failed to move a war powers resolution over the finish line last month that would have limited Trump’s authority in the country after Republicans accepted Rubio’s word that the U.S. would not use military force in the region.

The hearing also comes as the administration faces scrutiny over its continued strikes on alleged drug trafficking boats in the Caribbean. More than 120 people have been killed in the attacks since September.

The event is scheduled to begin at 10 a.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: 2 federal officers fired shots during Pretti encounter: CBP
data.description:
Two federal officers opened fire Saturday in the shooting that led to the death of 37-year-old Alex Pretti in Minneapolis, a Customs and Border Protection (CBP) official told Congress on Tuesday.

The official added that the shots were fired from Glock pistols as the agents struggled to take Pretti into custody, The Associated Press reported.

The incident over the weekend — the second fatal shooting of a protestor by immigration officials this month after Renee Good was killed by an Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agent on Jan. 7 — exacerbated tensions in the state fueled by President Trump’s surge of federal law enforcement to Minnesota amid his crackdown on illegal immigration.

Trump has largely defended his administration and Department of Homeland Security (DHS) Secretary Kristi Noem as blowback mounts over the two shootings. The president on Tuesday argued that Pretti should not have had a gun in his possession during the demonstrations.

“You can’t have guns. You can’t walk in with guns,” he told reporters, later calling Pretti’s fatal shooting “very unfortunate.”

A growing list of Democrats, including Sens. Ruben Gallego (D-Ariz.) and John Fetterman (D-Pa.) have called on Noem to resign or face impeachment over the incidents. The DHS chief has also faced scrutiny after she called Pretti a “domestic terrorist.”

“I think it’s ridiculous that someone called, you know, this poor victim a terrorist, a violent terrorist,” Gallego told MS NOW’s Katy Tur on Monday. “You know, that right there is impeachable.”

“The fact that she didn’t wait to see what the video that was coming out, automatically said that this man was brandishing a weapon, which we now know is not true,” he added.

Fetterman, a Centrist Democrat, also pressed Trump to fire Noem in a statement Tuesday.

“President Trump: I make a direct appeal to immediately fire Secretary Noem. Americans have died,” he wrote. “She is betraying DHS’s core mission and trashing your border security legacy.”

House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) and Democrats in the chamber’s Homeland Security Committee are also eyeing impeachment, especially if the party wins back the majority in the 2026 midterm elections.

data.title: Trump says Minneapolis mayor 'playing with fire'
data.description:
President Trump warned on Wednesday that Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey is “playing with fire” after saying the city will not enforce federal immigration laws. 

“Surprisingly, Mayor Jacob Frey just stated that, “Minneapolis does not, and will not, enforce Federal Immigration Laws.” This is after having had a very good conversation with him,” Trump wrote in a TruthSocial post. 

“Could somebody in his inner sanctum please explain that this statement is a very serious violation of the Law, and that he is PLAYING WITH FIRE!” Trump added. 

On Tuesday, Frey said he told White House border czar Tom Homan, who Trump dispatched to Minneapolis earlier this week, that the city will not enforce federal immigration laws. 

“I shared with Mr. Homan the serious negative impacts this operation has had on Minneapolis and surrounding communities, as well as the strain it has placed on our local police officers,” Frey wrote in a post on the social media platform X.

“I also made it clear that Minneapolis does not and will not enforce federal immigration laws, and that we will remain focused on keeping our neighbors and streets safe,” he continued. 

The mayor said that city leaders would keep in touch with Homan and his team. Gov. Tim Walz (D-Minn.) met with the border czar for a “productive” conversation earlier in the day after two U.S. citizens were killed by immigration authorities in Minneapolis. 

Trump administration officials, including Homan and Attorney General Pam Bondi, have implored local officials to comply with requests from Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) in an effort to detain migrants without legal status in the area.

data.title: Live updates: Senate tackles funding package amid DHS furor
data.description:
The Senate on Wednesday will work to advance a six-bill spending package, despite calls from Democrats to separate out Department of Homeland Security (DHS) funding after the deadly weekend shooting of a U.S. citizen in Minneapolis by federal immigration officials.

The incident has put Republicans on defense, and they are starting to splinter over support for DHS Secretary Kristi Noem. She retains the confidence of President Trump, however.

Overnight, Minnesota Rep. Ilhan Omar (D), who represents Minneapolis and some of its suburbs, was attacked at town hall in the city, as she called for Noem’s impeachment.

Trump on Wednesday morning will speak at a Treasury Department summit on his investment accounts for newborn babies.

Later, the Federal Reserve will announce its interest rate decision and chair Jerome Powell will deliver remarks. The central bank is expected to keep rates steady.

Follow live below.

data.title: China's latest war game uses hospital and science ships for espionage
data.description:
After the fall of Nicolas Maduro, there is was one absolutely clear loser: China. Militarily and politically, they suffered a devastating blow. Despite this, Beijing is not relinquishing its power and presence in Latin America. A military hospital ship and a scientific spy vessel will travel the region this month.

Concerns about China are neither unusual nor unfounded. The study “Surveying the Seas” by the Center for Strategic and International Studies highlights that China has a dual-use approach to oceanographic research. A clear example of this is that 80 percent of the 64 vessels operating in the Indian Ocean have a military geopolitical agenda.  

Beijing’s dual strategy is an integral part of civilian infrastructure programs and projects, including the port sector, telecommunications, energy, transportation and military hospital ships like the Silk Road Ark, which is traveling through Latin America. Yes, the same region that the United States declared is “our hemisphere.”

The Silk Road Ark is owned by the People’s Liberation Army Navy. Its tour across the region is part of Mission Harmony 2025-2026. Although its alleged purpose is health care, it also includes joint exercises and other activities with local militaries. 

The PLA military hospital is visiting 14 countries, including Jamaica, Barbados, Peru, Mexico, Brazil and Chile. The message seems to be very clear: China is here to offer an alternative to the traditional U.S. military partnership.  

Last week, the Silk Road Ark visited Uruguay, a nation governed by leftist leader Yamandú Orsi. A robust high-level delegation welcomed the PLA Navy ship, including Minister of Defense Sandra Lazo, National Defense Undersecretary Joel Rodríguez, Navy Chief José Elizondo and Chinese Ambassador Huan Yazhong. None of them belong to the health care sector. 

Following the events in Venezuela, China has taken some symbolic actions. The hospital ship, initially scheduled to visit 12 countries, expanded its itinerary to 14. Then, just days after President Trump announced his National Security Strategy, with an emphasis on Latin America, Beijing presented China’s Policy Paper on Latin America and the Caribbean — the third of its kind since 2016.

Earlier this month, China organized a military exercise in South Africa with some members of the BRICS group (Brazil, Russia, India, China and South Africa) and Iran. The show of force suffered a resounding defeat when India and Brazil failed to participate. This symbolic gesture was partly due to the Trump effect, which has fragmented China’s power in the Global South and Latin America.

But Beijing is doing more than just promoting the tour of a military hospital ship. A few days ago, the scientific vessel Tan Suo Yi Hao, denounced by an Australian researcher as a spy ship, arrived in Valparaiso, Chile. China presented the event as a harmless scientific expedition to strengthen relations with a country highly dependent on Beijing in key sectors such as telecommunications, transportation, energy and trade. 

In Brazil, the military hospital went above and beyond the health care façade. Members of the naval forces of both countries conducted a joint maritime exercise on search and rescue. China also made a demonstration of a combat and rescue operation. Authorities from the Regional Council of Medicine of the State of Rio de Janeiro complained about gaining access to inspect the ship or meet with the medical personnel on board. Once again, health care was not the main mission of the vessel.

The ship tour in Latin America and the so-called scientific vessel exposes the region’s economic and military vulnerabilities. Beijing is not promoting peace, health or scientific research, but rather a deep and complex military strategy that remains a threat to the U.S. and Latin America.  

China’s military ships and spy submarines should not be welcome in the Western Hemisphere. The U.S. needs to be aware of these events and proceed accordingly with those who engage in military activities with a U.S. enemy. 

Today, more than ever, the U.S. National Security Strategy is of paramount importance. The most powerful nation in the world needs to reassert and reinforce the Monroe Doctrine, to restore American preeminence in the Western Hemisphere and deny competitors the ability to position forces or other threatening capabilities in the region.

Now is the right time to take action.  

Arturo McFields is an exiled journalist, former Nicaraguan ambassador to the Organization of American States, and a former member of the Norwegian Peace Corps. He is an alumnus of the National Defense University’s Security and Defense Seminar and the Harvard Leadership course.
